posts.name: Noah C @ P U R 5 0
posts.cooked:
Hi everyone,

I’ve heard some vintages described as a “Drinker’s Vintage”. I’ve taken that to mean vintages that, while maybe not excellent and not praised in the press, nonetheless produced wines of good quality. Also, the term might describe a vintage that produced wines that are better for drinking in the short term than laying down for a long time. It seems that these represent a nice opportunity to buy wines that drink nicely but perhaps at a bit lower price than the more heralded vintages.

First, do you think the concept is a useful one when making purchasing decisions? If so, which ones do you think are drinkers vintages and in what region? 2001 and 2014 come to mind for Bordeaux. What about Burgundy, Piedmont, other places to look out for?

Thanks!

posts.name: Loren Supp
posts.cooked: while I have not yet tried the wines, many critics seem to be pointing at ‘21 bdx as just such a vintage. I feel like for a vintage to truly be a ‘drinkers vintage’, shouldn’t it have to de discounted when compared to more benchmark ones? ‘14 hits this nicely.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
2014 isn’t a very good example. It’s a very good vintage in both bdx and burgundy that will age well. The fact that it’s relatively inexpensive has more to do with the highly rated 15 and 16 vintages right afterwards. I’ve been buying up well priced 14s for a long time and prices have only started to tick up for drc/Rousseau and the first growths in the last 6 mo to a year. 14 drc rsv was ~1000 not that long ago.

In bdx, 01, 11, and the “off vintages” like 08, 12, and 13 would be better examples; in burgundy 07.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I tend to find the more interesting category to be what the French call “winemaker’s vintages”, where the best producers, thanks to superior agronomy and technical mastery, deliver genuinely great wines in average to below-average vintages. To me, wines such as 2002 Latour and Monfortino, 2006 whites from d’Auvenay, 2005 whites from Coche, etc etc exemplify this. Without the reassurance that the wine comes from a “great” vintage, wines such as this tend to be underrated by the press and the market, yet they are often just as, or more interesting than the banner years.

Don’t think 2008 got the memo about being an off vintage…

posts.name: Steve Costigan
posts.cooked: I’ve been really pleased with every 2011 red Burgundy I’ve opened, mostly Volnay.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked:
Michael might have a very high standard of what an “on” vintage is.

I’ve heard the term “drinker’s vintage” or similar used slightly differently, and implicitly referring to worse vintages than the ones suggested in the OP, i.e., vintages with wines not meant to be aged (long) or lacking the stuffing to require aging. The wines in these vintages can give pleasure in an early drinking window while the “great” vintages age, and are good for restaurant lists.

posts.name: Br1an Th0rne
posts.cooked: For me, many 1994 Bordeaux fall into this category, particularly from the left bank, and are drinking wonderfully today.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
Michael might have a very high standard of what an “on” vintage is.

I’ve heard the term “drinker’s vintage” or similar used slightly differently, and implicitly referring to worse vintages than the ones suggested in the OP, i.e., vintages with wines not meant to be aged (long) or lacking the stuffing to require aging. The wines in these vintages can give pleasure in an early drinking window while the “great” vintages age, and are good for restaurant lists.

I mean if you look at the last 15 vintages (05-20) the following bdx vintages are likely better than 08.

05, 09, 10, 14, 15, 16, 19, 20

You could argue about 17 and 18 as well.

Is a vintage in the bottom 1/2-1/3 considered on?

I think 14 is a very good vintage and shouldn’t be discussed in that context, fwiw.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Michael might have a very high standard of what an “on” vintage is.

I’ve heard the term “drinker’s vintage” or similar used slightly differently, and implicitly referring to worse vintages than the ones suggested in the OP, i.e., vintages with wines not meant to be aged (long) or lacking the stuffing to require aging. The wines in these vintages can give pleasure in an early drinking window while the “great” vintages age, and are good for restaurant lists.

I mean if you look at the last 15 vintages (05-20) the following bdx vintages are likely better than 08.

05, 09, 10, 14, 15, 16, 19, 20

You could argue about 17 and 18 as well.

Is a vintage in the bottom 1/2-1/3 considered on?

There are plenty of 2008s that are more vibrant and primary than their 2009 counterparts. We drank Le Pin last month and it remains an infant. In fact, if there is a criticism of the vintage it’s that the wines are still so young that very little complexity has emerged. Yet, while the wines remain quite tightly wound in many cases, they are holding more primary fruit than many 2005s. For me, 2008 is on a similar level to 2014, better than 2015. We will see where 2020 fits into the big picture now that the wines are in bottle.

posts.name: Chris_Rickey
posts.cooked: It seems to be cheating a bit to point to Le Pin, of all wines, as a vintage marker for 2008, particularly when Parker said, “2008 is an exceptionally strong vintage in Pomerol and St.-Emilion as well as Graves,” a comment he made after he had the benefit of having tasted both the 2009s and 2010s in barrel.

all jokes aside…completely agree. had a bunch of lafarge and d’angerville from 2011…all excellent.

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
‘drinkers vintage’ I’ve seen mostly to represent good short to medium term drinking, without being overly structured.
I’ve also seen a slight variant ‘English claret drinkers vintage’ to represent some of the less imposing / less opulent wines, that may stylistically be leaner than the modern norm. I guess on this site we’d use AFWE instead.

In restaurants with only current / recent vintage wines, the concept can be a useful one. At home less so, but then sometimes such wines are a good counterpoint to only having wines of a particular style.

posts.name: Ed Baum
posts.cooked:
I think about drinkers’ vintages being ones that mature early, taste of the place/are classical, and are less-heralded/less expensive.

In practice, examples for me are 2002 Bordeaux and 2007 Burgundy. Not nearly the concentration and length of great vintages, but often good value and taste like the wines are supposed to taste.

posts.name: Joel D@vid$on
posts.cooked:
I agree (mostly about 2001 for Bordeaux), also 2002 and 2004 come to mind.

Could argue that 2014 is one of these in Piedmont.

Also 2012 Champagne? These have been drinking really well from very early on, but also might have decently long lives.

posts.name: Matt Porter
posts.cooked:
Since you referenced italy, 2017 in Bolgheri is a great drinker’s vintage. Most are ready to drink. 2017 Guado al Tasso is great right now.

2017 in Piedmont is not a great drinkers vintage IMO just bc young nebbiolo doesn’t do it for me (save maybe a good gattinara).

Hi William

Not that I imagined we would agree, but I find 2015 to be dramatically better than 2008. Though 2008 is better for dry, white Bordeaux. I like many 2008 wines. Though 2008 is a firm, classic, discreet style that I do not always embrace. But I love numerous 2015 BDX, especially Margaux and Pomerol for all their richness, cashmere tannins, and sensuality.

posts.name: Jeff Leve
posts.cooked:
Hi everyone,

I’ve heard some vintages described as a “Drinker’s Vintage”. I’ve taken that to mean vintages that, while maybe not excellent and not praised in the press, nonetheless produced wines of good quality. Also, the term might describe a vintage that produced wines that are better for drinking in the short term than laying down for a long time. It seems that these represent a nice opportunity to buy wines that drink nicely but perhaps at a bit lower price than the more heralded vintages.

First, do you think the concept is a useful one when making purchasing decisions? If so, which ones do you think are drinkers vintages and in what region? 2001 and 2014 come to mind for Bordeaux. What about Burgundy, Piedmont, other places to look out for?

Thanks!

Noel… IMO, a drinker’s vintage is just that. It is a classic or light year that will provide pleasure in its youth, age a bit, and offer quality, but not extraordinary age-ability and it has very little upside potential to be resold at a profit. With modern vineyard management and the willingness to employ severe selection, placing more grapes into the second wines, there are fewer drinkers vintages from Bordeaux today. 2017, 2011, and perhaps 2012 are recent drinkers vintages.

As to the second part of your question about buying drinkers vintages, it depends on what is in your cellar, your disposable income, and personal taste. There are some folks on this board preferring less ripe vintages, so for them, those years are vintages to seek out.

09 and 10 are great vintages; I meant 08 is similar to 14 in that great vintages in 15 and 16 immediately followed it.

I also think 15 is a much better vintage than 14 or 08.

I’m sure many would disagree but I would rank the vintages something like:

16 ~ 10 > 15 ~ 09 ~ 19 ~ 05 > 14 ~ 18 > 17 > 08 > 12 ~ 13 ~ 07 ~ 06

posts.name: Justin Goodman
posts.cooked:
83 Bordeaux - overshadowed by hugely popular 82, but have had a few recently that were amazing. Margaux and LLC both come to mind that I’ve had in the last 6 months. LLC looks like you can steal it around ~150 at auction if you’re patient (and if you’re impatient at retail maybe another 50-60 bucks) … are you kidding me?

11 Napa - supposedly too lean and green, but great producers made great wines. Have scored some great values at auction from producers like Abreu, DV Maya, Harlan and VHR that are all drinking well. Had an 11 Madrona Ranch a couple weeks ago that was fantastic. Pretty sure I scooped it under 200 bucks at auction as part of a mixed lot.

posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
It seems to be cheating a bit to point to Le Pin, of all wines, as a vintage marker for 2008, particularly when Parker said, “2008 is an exceptionally strong vintage in Pomerol and St.-Emilion as well as Graves,” a comment he made after he had the benefit of having tasted both the 2009s and 2010s in barrel.

I wasn’t being disingenuous, it’s just the 2008 that I’ve most recent opened and drunk. It is also not a wine that evolves very slowly, so to comment on it being youthful is a bit more meaningful than saying e.g. “the 2008 Latour is still very youthful”.

I agree with RP that right bank and graves were especially strong in 2008, but the Médoc produced some real successes too. I suspect many, e.g. Montrose, will ultimately live up to RP’s often more optimistic barrel tasting notes rather than his more conservative from bottle ratings.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I mean if you look at the last 15 vintages (05-20) the following bdx vintages are likely better than 08.

05, 09, 10, 14, 15, 16, 19, 20

You could argue about 17 and 18 as well.

Is a vintage in the bottom 1/2-1/3 considered on?

There are plenty of 2008s that are more vibrant and primary than their 2009 counterparts. We drank Le Pin last month and it remains an infant. In fact, if there is a criticism of the vintage it’s that the wines are still so young that very little complexity has emerged. Yet, while the wines remain quite tightly wound in many cases, they are holding more primary fruit than many 2005s. For me, 2008 is on a similar level to 2014, better than 2015. We will see where 2020 fits into the big picture now that the wines are in bottle.

Hi William

Not that I imagined we would agree, but I find 2015 to be dramatically better than 2008. Though 2008 is better for dry, white Bordeaux. I like many 2008 wines. Though 2008 is a firm, classic, discreet style that I do not always embrace. But I love numerous 2015 BDX, especially Margaux and Pomerol for all their richness, cashmere tannins, and sensuality.

No surprise, indeed, but I find 2015s a bit soft. Many also stand out as evolving quite rapidly in the context of verticals of recent vintages. For me, there are so many places in the world that do a very good job making rich, sweet-fruited, accessible red wines from Bordeaux grape varieties that I look for something more than that from Bordeaux itself. So years such as 2015 and 2018 are not vintages I gravitate towards, whereas 2016 and 2019 are much more to my taste.

However, to return to the topic at hand, what we can agree on, I think, is that categorizing 2008 as an “off vintage” in a list including 2007 and 2013 isn’t fair to the quality of the 2008s, which was the point I was making.

posts.name: Mike Leigh
posts.cooked: 2011 and 2014 Norther Rhones. 2011 Loire reds aren’t too shabby either.

posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.cooked:
Each to his own, but I find ranking vintages a bit of a pointless exercise. Of course some vintages produce riper richer bigger wines and others produce wines that less ripe and rich etc etc.

I think every vintage has a story to tell and every vintage (after 1993 in Bdx and after 2004 in Burg) has produced really good wines in decent quantities. I prefer to celebrate vintage diversity and there are wines I buy every year irrespective of vintage rating. The idea we can rank vintages is a bit of an over simplification and not terribly useful for my buying decisions.

The so called great vintages may be the best for long term ageing but that is only one of many metrics by which to judge a vintage. Sometimes the so called “lesser” vintages are better at the table and match better with a wider range of foods that the bigger more powerful vin de garde wines from “great vintages”

As an example, Michael ranked 2006 last in his Bdx relative ranking. I had the 2006 Ch. Clos Eglise last week and thought it was excellent. This illustrates (for me anyway) the limitions of vintage ranking exercises.

Brodie

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked:
Each to his own, but I find ranking vintages a bit of a pointless exercise. Of course some vintages produce riper richer bigger wines and others produce wines that less ripe and rich etc etc.

I think every vintage has a story to tell and every vintage (after 1993 in Bdx and after 2004 in Burg) has produced really good wines in decent quantities. I prefer to celebrate vintage diversity and there are wines I buy every year irrespective of vintage rating. The idea we can rank vintages is a bit of an over simplification and not terribly useful for my buying decisions.

The so called great vintages may be the best for long term ageing but that is only one of many metrics by which to judge a vintage. Sometimes the so called “lesser” vintages are better at the table and match better with a wider range of foods that the bigger more powerful vin de garde wines from “great vintages”

As an example, Michael ranked 2006 last in his Bdx relative ranking. I had the 2006 Ch. Clos Eglise last week and thought it was excellent. This illustrates (for me anyway) the limitions of vintage ranking exercises.

Brodie

Much to agree with here.

  • It’s a clumsy tool, as it ignores the site specific variations, variation in viticulture & picking dates, approach in the winery. All very significant factors, such that putting a number on a whole region is crude (immensely so for something like ‘South Australia’, which is huge).
  • Personal taste can lead to some ratings that I find bizarre e.g. the love of some critics for 1997 and 2000 in Barolo / Barbaresco resulted in vintage ‘scores’ that I found preposterous.
  • When I’m cellaring wine, I’m typically in for the long haul, even though like many I initially was drinking everything rather early. With cellar maturity (and a significant dabbling in the auction scene to fast track some maturity), it allowed me to cellar as long as I wanted to. Thus for me, cellaring is mostly focused on the ‘vin de garde’ style, but nonetheless I’m not rigidly stuck to that, and each bottle typically gets cellared for a suitable (I hope), but not prescriptive length of time.
  • There’s certainly joy for me in edging away from the lauded warmer vintages, especially in some regions that already push the ripeness / alcohol levels. If that means I get the wines better suited to my tastes for cheaper… wahaaayyyyy!
  • Wines to fit the mood / food is something I very much agree with. Most commonly this is a more crude decision for me e.g. saving Aussie Shiraz, even from ‘cooler’ regions for winter, but something lighter like a Bulles de Miniere (sparkling Loire cab franc) for summer / picnics. However yes with enough vintage variation (e.g. 2002 vs 2003 in the Langhe) that would absolutely come into my thinking.
  • It’s useful to know ‘broadly’ what a particular vintage may bring for a wine (intense, tight, light, green etc.), but it’s really just a rough indication that can be trumped by experience with a particular wine.
  • Our rush to put numbers on things to rank them is especially stupid with something as personal as taste in wine.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
For Burgundy, I think of 2017 as a drinkers vintage. It is not an off vintage - in fact, I like it very much. Rather, I think of it as a drinkers vintage because you can drink a whole lot of them now with a lot of pleasure. Great restaurant vintage. For example, I had a 2017 Domaine Heitz-Lochardet - Armand Heitz Clos des Poitures at a restaurant in May. TNs: rdj, heitz-lochardet, Clos des papes - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers I don’t think of Pommard as a village producing a whole lot of wines to drink early. But, I thought this one was beautiful.

I remember going to Burgundy in 2013, finding a lot of 2007s on wine lists in restaurants and finding a lot of pleasure from the wines (esp. the Volnays). If I went to those same restaurants now, I would be hoping there would be 2017s on the wine lists.

I think 14 is a very good vintage and shouldn’t be discussed in that context, fwiw.

I will be blasted for this but for my palate 2009 is an off vintage in Bordeaux. Too many wines taste cooked.

Not sure what wines you’re trying. I’ve had all the first growths this year and they’ve been spectacular, obviously young. VCC was superb as well.

Don’t drink a lot anymore because I have not liked many of the ones I have tasted. Over the years I have had Cos D’Estournal, Montrose, Figeac, and Leoville Poyferre, among others. I esp. found the Leoville Poyferre to taste stewed. Maybe these wines have improved since I had them. Maybe the first growths and VCC were better. But, from what I have tasted, this is not my style of vintage.

posts.name: P eter L owet
posts.cooked:
I think about drinkers’ vintages being ones that mature early, taste of the place/are classical, and are less-heralded/less expensive.

In practice, examples for me are 2002 Bordeaux and 2007 Burgundy. Not nearly the concentration and length of great vintages, but often good value and taste like the wines are supposed to taste.



‘drinkers vintage’ I’ve seen mostly to represent good short to medium term drinking, without being overly structured.
I’ve also seen a slight variant ‘English claret drinkers vintage’ to represent some of the less imposing / less opulent wines, that may stylistically be leaner than the modern norm. I guess on this site we’d use AFWE instead.

In restaurants with only current / recent vintage wines, the concept can be a useful one. At home less so, but then sometimes such wines are a good counterpoint to only having wines of a particular style.

IMO Ed and Ian have it right. We are not talking about OFF-vintages like 2007 Bordeaux. We’re also not talking about big-styled, controversial vintages like 2003 Burgundy. And I don’t think we’re not talking about vintages like 2008 Bordeaux (or to my taste 1998 Burgundy) which were extremely well-priced, but not styled like a drinkers/restaurant vintage.

It’s of course a plus when a drinker’s vintage is priced like an off-vintage. That was the case for 2000/2007 Burgundy so I wish I had purchased much more of those.

participants.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
